learning_outcomes.text: Solve first- and higher-order ordinary differential equations analytically and numerically.
Analyze linear systems using eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and fundamental matrices.
Formulate and solve boundary value problems, including Sturm-Liouville problems.
Apply PDE methods to model practical engineering and science problems (heat, wave, Laplace equations).
Implement MATLAB solutions for ODE and PDE problems.

policies.summary: Homework assignments are due at the end of each class period. No late homework accepted after 24 hours. Discussion with peers is encouraged, but solutions must be written independently. Work must be readable and show all derivation details.

Students must follow the University stipulated attendance policy. Attendance will be taken at the beginning of each virtual class. Students are responsible for timely attendance and informing the instructor of supported excuses.
Students must remain in the virtual classroom during a class meeting and are not allowed to come in and out unless due to a network issue.

Cell phones must be in silent/manner mode during virtual class. Specific calculators (TI-82, 83, or 84 series) are allowed during exams; all other electronic devices (cell phones, iPads, laptops, other graphing calculators) are considered cheating on exams.

Students are expected to uphold standards of intellectual and academic integrity. This includes policies against plagiarism (presenting another's work as one's own), cheating (unauthorized assistance), unauthorized collaboration (substantial joint effort without consent), falsification (misrepresenting or fabricating information), and multiple submissions (submitting the same work for credit more than once without consent).
Any student found in violation of academic honesty will fail the course. Cases may be investigated by the professor or Academic Dean.
Student appeals regarding academic dishonesty can be filed with the Student Review Committee through the Office of the Academic Dean.

The university is committed to providing reasonable accommodations to students with disabilities in accordance with federal legislation (Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA). Students are responsible for informing faculty of their need and providing authorized documentation. Contact the instructor personally by the end of the first week of classes for discussion on accommodations.
Students should refer to the ECSU Viking Compass Website for the most updated COVID-19 policies and information.

recommendations.title: AI as an Explanatory Assistant
recommendations.description: Encourage students to use AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Wolfram Alpha) to gain deeper conceptual understanding of complex topics in differential equations, explore alternative explanations, or clarify difficult mathematical concepts. Students should be reminded that AI might hallucinate or provide incorrect answers, requiring critical evaluation.
risks: Over-reliance on AI for understanding without developing own critical thinking
Potential for AI to provide incorrect or misleading information
mitigations: Require students to verify AI outputs with course materials or instructor
Integrate discussions on AI use and limitations into classroom activities
Assign tasks that require human critical analysis beyond AI capabilities

recommendations.title: AI for Code Debugging and Optimization
recommendations.description: Allow students to use AI tools to help debug their MATLAB code for projects and homework, or to suggest ways to optimize existing code. This leverages AI for a practical, skill-development purpose without solving the core mathematical problem for them.
risks: Students might rely on AI to write code from scratch, hindering their own coding skill development
Potential for AI to introduce errors or inefficient solutions if not carefully guided
mitigations: Set clear guidelines on permissible AI assistance for code generation vs. debugging
Require students to explain the bugs identified and the AI's role in their resolution
Focus assessments on understanding the code and mathematical principles rather than just output

recommendations.title: AI-Generated Practice and Step-by-Step Guidance
recommendations.description: Students can use AI to generate additional practice problems on specific topics (e.g., first-order ODEs, Laplace transforms) or to provide step-by-step solutions to problems they've already attempted, acting as a private tutor. This reinforces learning without direct assessment manipulation.
risks: Over-reliance on AI for solutions without internalizing the problem-solving process
Potential for AI to generate flawed problems or incorrect solutions
mitigations: Advise students to use this after attempting problems themselves and to critically evaluate AI-generated steps
Instructors could provide curated AI prompts for effective practice
Emphasize the importance of understanding the 'why' behind each step

recommendations.title: AI for Pedagogical Support
recommendations.description: Instructors can use AI tools to brainstorm lesson plans, generate diverse examples for problem sets, or draft quiz questions aligned with learning outcomes. This can enhance teaching efficiency and creativity.
risks: Over-reliance reducing instructor's own pedagogical innovation
mitigations: Instructor must critically review and adapt all AI-generated content
Use AI as a starting point for ideas, not a final product
Ensure AI outputs align with course specificities and student needs

recommendations.title: Develop and Communicate Clear AI Usage Policy
recommendations.description: The instructor should develop a clear, explicit policy on the acceptable and unacceptable uses of AI in the course, covering all assessments (homework, projects, exams) and study habits. This policy should be clearly communicated at the start of the semester.
risks: Ambiguity in policy leading to unintentional violations
Students feeling overly restricted or confused about AI use
mitigations: Provide specific examples of permissible and impermissible AI use
Host a dedicated Q&A session at the beginning of the semester
Integrate the AI policy into the existing academic honesty guidelines

recommendations.title: Leveraging AI for Enhanced Accessibility
recommendations.description: Encourage students with language barriers or certain learning disabilities to use AI tools for translation, text-to-speech, or simplification of complex texts, helping them access course material more effectively.
risks: Inaccurate translations or simplifications impacting understanding
Privacy concerns with sensitive student information if shared with AI tools
mitigations: Recommend trusted and privacy-conscious AI tools
Advise against inputting personally identifiable information into AI systems
Emphasize AI as a support, not a replacement for fundamental learning

recommendations.title: Adapting Assessments for the AI Era
recommendations.description: Design assessments that minimize the ability for AI to simply 'solve' them, focusing on higher-order thinking, critical analysis, unique problem contexts, oral presentations, or requiring personal reflection and integration of multiple sources.
risks: Increased instructor workload in designing novel assessments
Difficulty in designing truly 'AI-proof' tasks without compromising learning objectives
Potential for limiting traditional quantitative problem-solving practice
mitigations: Incorporate in-class, proctored components for key parts of assessments
Require detailed explanations of reasoning and methodology, not just final answers
Use current events, personalized data, or specific course readings in problems that AI cannot easily generalize

recommendations.title: Foster Ethical AI Dialogue
recommendations.description: Integrate discussions on the ethical implications of AI use in academic settings, including issues of intellectual property, bias, data privacy, and the role of human intellect in an AI-augmented world. This prepares students for responsible AI use beyond the classroom.
risks: Discussions might take valuable time away from subject matter content
Differing student opinions on AI ethics could lead to complex classroom dynamics
mitigations: Dedicate specific, limited time for these discussions within the course schedule
Provide relevant articles or case studies for students to review beforehand
Link ethical considerations directly to the course's academic integrity policies
